title: 3 Legal Considerations
body_text:
3	Legal Considerations
3.1	Overview of Legal Considerations
Credit unions are responsible for complying with applicable legislation and regulations. The information provided in this Chapter contains a summary of the main statutes and regulations that pertain to this Part and is provided for convenience only. The material in this Chapter isnГўВЂВ™t intended to take the place of the statutes and regulations but rather to provide a summary to assist the credit union. Reference should be made to the actual statute or regulation for the full text. Note that there is no guarantee of accuracy for the online statutes.
3.2	CPA Rules
The Canadian Payments Association (CPA) develops and implements the rules that apply to the clearing and settlement of different types of payments between its member financial institutions. Refer to the CPA website for more information on CPA Rules.
CPA Rule A1, General Rules Pertaining to Items Acceptable for Exchange, for the Purpose of Clearing and Settlement
ГўВЂВ“ Outlines the general requirements for processing Canadian dollar items and establishes the amount limit for paper items. Paper items that equal or exceed $25 million cannot be exchanged through the Clearing on the same day.
CPA Rule A4, Returned and Redirected Items
ГўВЂВ“ Outlines the dishonour and return of items, including return reasons and time frames.
CPA Rule A10, Images, Image Printouts and Return Replacement Documents
ГўВЂВ“ Outlines the requirements applicable to the creation and storage of images, the use of image printouts or Return Replacement Documents for the purpose of return. Cheque images have the same legal standing as do physical cleared cheques.
CPA Rule K1, Bulk Exchange and Settlement of U.S. Dollar Paper Items
ГўВЂВ“ Provides information on the exchange and settlement, in Canada, of US dollar items.
CPA Standards 006, Specifications for MICR-Encoded Payment Items
ГўВЂВ“ Defines the location and background design of the necessary data elements for imageable MICR-encoded payment items and other MICR encoded payment items.
For more information see the Cheques Part.
Related Information
Section 1.3
, Contacts and Websites (Canadian Payments Association)
3.3	Bills of Exchange Act
The
Bills of Exchange Act (BEA)
governs the form of and dealings with financial instruments such as bills of exchange, cheques, and promissory notes. The
BEA
regulates financial issues such as the time permitted for the dishonour and return of cheques, forged endorsements, and rights and powers of bills of exchange holders.
The
BEA
states that ГўВЂВњan Official Image of an eligible bill may be dealt with and used for all purposes as though it were the eligible bill.ГўВЂВќ
END
